name: Act 2 — The Deep Road (Director Journal, Part 2)

text.content:
Level: 4–5
Duration: 1–2 sessions
Location: The Ivory Tower (row 17, col 31); the Keepers' archive
Key NPCs: Kaelen (discovering her destiny), ancient Keeper spirits (optional), Dreyfus's lingering presence
Type: Exploration and investigation, minor encounter (guardian construct or Wyrd-haunting), lore revelation, character revelation, preparation
Tone: Awe and dread; the weight of knowledge; a meeting with the past; the moment Kaelen understands what is being asked of her

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 44 | Level: 3 | VP into Level: 12

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 44 | Level: 3 | VP into Level: 12

Overview

The Ivory Tower is an ancient, bone-white structure built from the bones of some long-dead creature (a Jotun? A proto-world entity?). It stands at the edge of the Bone-Fields, a solitary landmark of pale stone against the white snow.

It was built by the original Keepers as an archive—a repository of their knowledge, their rites, their understanding of how to work with Yggdrasil and the Ever-Embers.

The tower is abandoned now. One room shows evidence of violence—one of the Six Keepers died here months ago, killed by Dreyfus before he went after the rest. The archive is partially corrupted by the Rot, partially preserved by the Keepers' wards.

Most importantly, the tower contains records that will make Kaelen understand what the Emberwell requires of her. Not all the details—the party cannot piece together the full picture—but enough for Kaelen to suspect, and enough for her to become quietly terrified.

By the end of this beat, Kaelen knows (or strongly suspects) that she is meant to become the new Ever-Ember. She will not share this knowledge fully. She will carry it alone, as the weight grows heavier with every mile north.

Part 1: The Approach to the Tower

Geography and Appearance

The Ivory Tower rises from a rocky prominence about 3 miles northeast of the party's position in the Bone-Fields. From a distance, it is pale—almost white—against the dark sky. As the party approaches, they can see:

  • The tower itself is a single, elegant spiraling structure, perhaps 60 feet tall. It has no windows in the lower half, only stone. Higher up, small apertures suggest windows or vents. At the very top, a flat platform with what might be a beacon.
  • The tower is clearly built from enormous bones—ribs, vertebrae, other structures the party doesn't immediately recognize. They've been worked, carved, fitted together with precision. It's architecture made from a corpse.
  • At the base is a small enclosure—walls of fitted stone, perhaps a few rooms or storage chambers. These are more recent (built within the last 500 years), not as ancient as the tower itself.
  • Smoke has not risen from this place in months. But as the party approaches, they can feel something—a presence, a lingering warmth in the stones themselves, a sense that this place is not entirely dead.

Entering the Tower

The door is carved from stone and is heavy. It's not locked, but it's stiff from disuse. Once the party pushes it open, they are in a small vestibule. The air here is cool but not cold. The party will need to use torches or light sources.

The door opens onto darkness.

But as you step inside, something shifts. The stone beneath your feet is warm. Not hot, but warmth—real, felt warmth—spreading through the soles of your boots. And if you look closely, you can see faint lines carved into the floor, glowing with a soft blue light. Wards. Ancient wards, still active, still burning with something that might be the last spark of Keeper magic.

The vestibule is small, octagonal, with passages leading in four directions. Above, the tower rises. You can see spiral stairs ascending into shadow.

And on the floor, in the center of the room: scorch marks. Black, charred stone. And bones—human bones, scattered and broken. Some of them show the marks of violence—claw marks, burn marks, the kind of damage that comes from a fierce struggle.

Someone died here. Not long ago. Within the last few months, perhaps.

Kaelen approaches the bones slowly. She kneels beside them and is very still.

"She was a Keeper," Kaelen says quietly. "I can feel it. The wards are responding to her death. They're still mourning. The tower itself is mourning."

Guardian Encounter (Optional)

Victory Points — Type: Combat (Optional - triggered only if party deceives the construct) | Difficulty: Easy | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

Victory Points — Type: Combat (Optional - triggered only if party deceives the construct) | Difficulty: Easy | VP Award: 1 VP on victory

The party may encounter a guardian construct or a Wyrd-haunting here. This should be minor—not a full combat, but a test or a challenge that can be overcome through negotiation, understanding, or a single combat encounter.

Option 1: Guardian Construct Encounter

Minor Encounter

A stone figure, humanoid, carved in the style of ancient Keeper craftsmanship. It was created to defend the archive. It is half-corrupted by the Rot—part of its stone face is blackened and cracking.

It does not attack immediately. It simply bars the way, watching. A moderate Presence test will reveal that it is asking a question—not in words, but in the pressure of its gaze: "Are you a Keeper? Are you a friend of the Keepers?"

  • If answered truthfully (Kaelen is a Keeper): The construct steps aside. Passage is granted.
  • If deceived: The construct attacks (easy combat, 1 round, then the party can retreat or overcome it).

Use the @UUID[Actor.pCvMQr6nRUKsXK8F]{Keeper Guardian Construct} stat block if combat occurs.

Option 2: Wyrd-Haunting Encounter

Atmospheric Encounter

A trapped emotional echo. The last moments of the slain Keeper, replaying.

The party sees, in translucent form, a woman (Keeper Soren) being attacked by something with golden eyes and terrible claws (Dreyfus in his true form). The vision is not violent toward the party—it's simply replaying over and over.

A moderate Presence test will allow the party to understand what they're witnessing and to move past it without harm. Kaelen, if she witnesses this, becomes very still. She recognizes the form attacking—it matches Gragnir's description of Dreyfus.

📝 Recommendation

Use Option 1 or 2. They add atmosphere and poignancy without requiring a full combat. They also emphasize that the tower is a place of loss.

Part 2: Exploring the Archive

The Tower's Layout

The tower has several levels:

Ground level: The vestibule (where they entered). Passages lead to:

  • West passage: Storage rooms. Mostly empty. Some ancient sealed containers (unopened for centuries).
  • North passage: The violence chamber. Evidence of Dreyfus's attack. The bones of the slain Keeper.
  • East passage: Stairs descending to an underground chamber (flooded, not accessible without swimming or wading through cold water).
  • South passage: Stairs spiraling upward into the tower.

Upper levels: (Accessed by the spiral stairs)

  • Second level: More storage. Shelves lined with tablets, scrolls (mostly preserved, some damaged).
  • Third level: A reading chamber. Desks, tablets organized by theme. Windows here provide light (if the party arrives during daylight).
  • Fourth level: The primary archive. This is the heart of the tower. Thousands of tablets. Careful organization. Records of Keeper knowledge, rites, histories.
  • Fifth level: The beacon chamber. An open platform. A view of the surrounding land. It is very cold here.

The Violence Chamber (North Passage)

The party will likely encounter this first. It is disturbing and informative.

The north passage opens into a larger chamber—perhaps 20 feet across, round, with a domed ceiling. The floor is covered in scorch marks, blackened stone, and debris.

In the center, scattered across the floor: bones. Human bones, but also something else. Claw marks gouged into the stone itself—deep, deliberate claw marks that suggest something with strength far beyond human.

There are tablets here, scattered and broken. Some are inscribed with text that Kaelen recognizes as Keeper runes.

And on one wall, written in ash or soot: a symbol. A claw mark, so large that it must have been made by something standing 8+ feet tall. It's a message, or a claim. A mark left by something that wanted to be known.

Kaelen kneels beside the scattered bones. Her hand shakes as she picks up a tablet—one of the few that's still relatively intact.

She reads it silently, her face growing paler with each moment. When she sets it down, she doesn't look at the party.

"Dreyfus was here. He killed her—Soren, her name was. Soren Keeper. She came to the archive to hide the records, to preserve them from something. But Dreyfus found her. He tracked her here."

Kaelen's voice is very quiet.

"He carved his mark on the wall. A message to whoever came after. 'I was here. I know what you're protecting.'"

⚠ Director Secret: Evidence of Dreyfus's Knowledge

  1. Dreyfus was here months ago (when he was killing the other Keepers—consistent with Act 1 lore).
  2. Soren died defending the archive, though she failed to stop Dreyfus.
  3. Dreyfus knows what's in the tower. He's seen the records. He's told Grafvitnir everything.
  4. The tower was a sanctuary, but it wasn't safe. Nowhere is safe from Grafvitnir's agents.

The Ward-Oath (Fourth Level Threshold)

The staircase to the fourth level is intact, but the landing at the top is not empty. The air here is different — warmer, drier, and it hums with a low vibration that Kaelen recognizes immediately.

The stairs end at a broad landing. The door to the fourth level is open — it has no physical lock, no bar, no mechanism. But the air in front of it shimmers like heat-haze over summer stone.

Kaelen stops. She raises a hand, not to touch the shimmer, but to feel it.

"Keeper wards. Active ones." Her voice carries a mixture of relief and dread. "The archive has been sealed since Soren died. The wards survived because they don't need a living Keeper to maintain them — they were carved into the stone itself, powered by the tower's residual heat."

As the party approaches, the shimmer intensifies. A sound fills the landing — not a voice, exactly, but the impression of words, like reading text carved in light:

I who enter this archive swear that the knowledge within shall be used only to restore and defend the Ever-Embers. I shall not bear this knowledge as a weapon against the living. I shall not share what I learn with those who serve the Grave-Wolf or his agents. What I take from this place, I carry as a trust, not a tool.

The words hang in the air. Waiting.

Kaelen recognizes the protocol. "The old Keepers built this into every major archive. You cannot enter without speaking the oath aloud. The wards will know if you're lying — they read intent, not just words."

If the party speaks the oath aloud and means it: The shimmer parts like a curtain. The archive opens.

If the party refuses or tries to deceive the wards: The shimmer solidifies into a barrier of pale heat. No passage. Kaelen can still access fragments from the lower levels — scattered tablets, incomplete records — but the full archive, including the First Kindling rite, remains sealed.

📝 Director Note: The Archive Oath's Constraints

This oath has teeth. It constrains the party in specific, meaningful ways:

  • "Not as a weapon against the living": The Pale Maw's soldiers are living people — not undead, not monsters. If the party learns tactical information from the archive (troop movements, supply routes, Grafvitnir's plans), the oath prevents them from using it to harm living soldiers. They can defend themselves if attacked, but they cannot weaponize Keeper intelligence against mortal armies. This forces creative problem-solving in Act 3.
  • "Not share with those who serve the Grave-Wolf": Straightforward, but note — the party may not always know who serves Grafvitnir. Dreyfus fooled them once. The oath punishes carelessness as well as betrayal.
  • Word economy: Breaking the archive oath costs 2 Word permanently and causes the knowledge itself to become unreliable — facts blur, details shift, as if the wards are reaching across distance to reclaim what was taken in bad faith. This is a narrative consequence, not just a mechanical one.

If the party swore Gragnir's Root-Oath in Beat 8, the archive oath reinforces it — both prioritize Yggdrasil's renewal over political advantage. If they didn't swear the Root-Oath, the archive oath stands alone as a check on how they use Keeper knowledge.

The Primary Archive (Fourth Level)

This is where Kaelen makes her discovery.

The fourth level is a vast circular chamber — the inside of the tower is larger than the outside suggests, which means it extends partway underground or has some spatial oddity created by Keeper craft.

The walls are lined with shelves. The shelves are covered with tablets: thousands of them, organized by theme and by era. Some are ancient (carved in archaic Keeper runes that Kaelen barely recognizes). Some are recent (within the last 100 years).

There is a writing desk in the center of the chamber. Recent work is scattered there — tablets in progress, notes, a half-finished chronicle.

Kaelen's Discovery

If the party gives Kaelen time to explore (at least an hour), she will find something specific.

She will locate a section labeled—in careful script—The First Kindling: The Original Rite.

This is a text that Kaelen has only read in abbreviated form. But here, in the archive, is the full version. Original, carved by one of the First Keepers.

Archive Fragment: The Willing Anchor

From The First Kindling: The Original Rite

The original rite describes the process of creating the Ever-Embers. It requires:

  1. A source of flame—something from Muspelheim itself, or touched by Muspelheim's fire.
  2. A living anchor—a human being who is willing to join with the flame, to become its vessel.
  3. A binding oath—witnessed by the World-Tree and the spirits of the land.

The text describes what happens to the anchor:

"The one who takes the flame into their body becomes the Ever-Ember. They do not die, but they do not live as others live. They become the hearth itself—their warmth is the world's warmth. Their breath is the fire's breath. If they are wounded, the fire flickers. If they are desperate, the world grows cold. Their life is not their own; it belongs to all who shelter by the flame."

"The anchor does not leave their post. The first anchor, Sigrid, remained in the deep chambers of the first tower for 300 years. She did not age. She did not weaken. She simply was. She was the Ever-Ember, and as long as she stood at her post, the world was warm.

"When at last Sigrid could no longer maintain the flame—whether from weariness or from choice, the records do not say—she released the fire to a successor. And she transformed. Her body became stone. Her warmth became the warmth of the hearth-chamber itself. She gave everything to the anchor that followed."

"A Keeper must understand this from the first day of training: you are learning to be a witness and a guardian, not a wielder. The flame does not serve the Keeper. The Keeper serves the flame. And when the time comes, if it comes, you may be asked to give more than your service. You may be asked to give yourself."

Marginal note in recent hand:

"Kaelen came to us with the false shame of warming one village. But she does not understand what she was created for. The tree has marked her. The fire knows her name. She will understand, in time, that her exile was not a punishment. It was a preparation. When the Emberwell calls, she must answer. Not because we ask. But because she is the last. And the last must give everything."

Kaelen's Reaction

When Kaelen finishes reading this, she is quiet for a very long time.

Her hands are shaking. Her face is pale. She sets the tablet down very carefully, as if it might shatter.

Then she stands, and without speaking, she goes to the window and looks out toward the north. Toward Surt's Forge. Toward the Emberwell.

If the party asks her what she's found, she does not answer immediately. When she does, her voice is measured but tight:

"The Keeper training texts say that the anchor—the one who becomes the Ever-Ember—doesn't leave their post. They stay, and they keep the world warm. I thought they meant it metaphorically. I thought it was ritual."

She turns back to the party.

"But they meant it literally. The first Keeper, Sigrid, stood in the same place for 300 years. Without moving. Her body became stone. She became the hearth itself."

Kaelen's voice is very steady now, but underneath there is fear:

"That's what the Emberwell is. It's not a place where you light a flame and it burns forever. It's a place where someone stands, and as long as they stand there, the flame burns. And when they can't anymore... they give it to someone else."

She does not say the next part explicitly. But it hangs in the air:

And I am the last Keeper. And Yggdrasil is dying. So when I reach the Emberwell, I will have to be the one who stands.

⚠ Director Secret: Kaelen's Layer 2 Realization

Kaelen does NOT tell the party the full implication. She suspects. She understands. But she does not share what she now knows. She carries this knowledge alone, as her burden.

If the party presses her, she might say: "I need to think about what I've read. I need to understand the rite completely before we go further. But everything I've been trained for... I think I finally understand what I've been trained for."

And then she becomes quiet, withdrawn. She may not sleep well that night.

This is the moment when Kaelen transitions from reluctant guide to someone carrying an impossible burden. The party should sense the change but not fully understand it.

Part 3: Other Discoveries in the Archive

Maps and Records

The archive contains:

  1. Maps of the Emberwell approach: Detailed maps showing the route from Surt's Forge down into the Emberwell chamber. This is useful for Beats 18-19. The party can copy or memorize these.
  2. Records of previous Keepers: Biographical sketches of the Seven Keepers, going back centuries. Kaelen will recognize some of their names from her training. She will realize that the tradition goes much deeper and longer than she thought.
  3. The administrative history: Records of how the Keepers maintained their order, how they trained successors, how they kept the rites alive. This is useful context but not immediately actionable.
  4. Evidence of Dreyfus's presence: Claw marks on some tablets, scorch marks on others. A few tablets are destroyed beyond reading. Kaelen can determine that Dreyfus read the archive before destroying parts of it. He took knowledge. He learned.

The Unfinished Chronicle

The writing desk holds a half-finished chronicle written in what appears to be Soren Keeper's hand. It dates from the weeks before her death.

⚠ Director Secret: Soren's Final Words

Excerpts from the unfinished chronicle:

"The Rot spreads faster now. I have felt it moving through the deep roots. It is not random. There is intelligence directing it. Grafvitnir's work, surely.

"I came to the archive to preserve the records of the First Kindling. If the end comes—if the Keepers fall and the flame dies—someone must know how it was done. Someone must know what was sacrificed.

"There is a note in the oldest texts, written by Sigrid herself: 'When the world needs renewal, the last Keeper will know what to do.' Sigrid lived 300 years. I wonder if she knew she was the last.

"Kaelen is still in exile in her grove. I have not told her the truth—that we do not blame her, that she was not as broken as she believed. I wanted her to have peace for at least a few more years.

"But if Grafvitnir continues to move as he is moving, we may not have years. The root-anchor in the north may fall within months. And if the northern anchor fails, the Emberwell will open.

"That is Kaelen. It has always been Kaelen. She is the last. The tree chose her. And she must choose what comes next."

What the party understands:

  1. Soren knew the end was coming.
  2. Soren knew that Kaelen was destined for something.
  3. Soren died trying to preserve knowledge for whoever would come after.

This is heavy. This is funeral rites for someone the party never met, but who died trying to help them.

Part 4: Rest and Preparation

The Warmth of the Tower

The tower, despite being abandoned, is warm. The Keeper wards maintain a temperature that is, while cold by summer standards, genuinely heated compared to the outside world.

The party can rest here safely. Heat cost is reduced significantly (the tower provides natural warmth). If they rest for the night, they can recover Stamina and prepare for the final push north.

What the Tower Provides

  • Preserved food: In sealed containers in the storage rooms. Dried fish, salted meat, dried fruits. Enough to provision the party for several more days.
  • Alchemical preparations: Medicines, stimulants, potions. Useful for recovery and for pushing through exhaustion.
  • Writing materials: If anyone wants to leave a message or a record.
  • Historical texts: The party can take tablets if they wish, but weight becomes an issue.

The Keepers were prepared for long-term isolation. They left provisions.

Optional: Leaving a Message

The party might decide to leave a message in the archive. If they do, make it meaningful. What do they want to tell whoever comes after?

Some possibilities:

  • "Kaelen Keeper has passed this way. She carries the hope of Svellheim north. If she succeeds, the world continues. If she fails... remember that she tried."
  • "We have read Soren's chronicle. We understand what she died trying to preserve. Kaelen knows now. The burden is heavy, but she is strong."

Messages can be brief or detailed. They become part of the historical record of the campaign.

Part 5: The Departure

Kaelen's Final Words

As the party prepares to leave the tower and continue north, Kaelen may take a moment at the threshold to look back.

"The Keepers spent their lives guarding these records, guarding the knowledge. They died, one by one, and no one came. Until now. Until us."

She pauses, then:

"We have to succeed. Not just for Hrafnborg. Not just for the people of Svellheim. For them. For Soren, for the ones who came before her, for everyone who ever stood at the hearth and kept the flame alive. We have to reach the Emberwell. We have to renew the spark."

This is as close as she comes to admitting what she now knows—that the renewal might cost her everything.

Pointing North

From the tower's height, the party can see north. The landscape gradually rises, becoming more mountainous. In the distance (if the weather is clear), they might see the distant glow of Surt's Forge—a warm amber light in the frozen darkness.

Surt's Forge is perhaps 40-50 miles away. 3-4 days of hard travel.

Beyond it, hidden, is the Emberwell.

The party's journey is entering its final phase.

Beat 15 — Victory Point Summary

No mandatory VP encounters. The guardian is optional, triggered only by deception.

Ending Beat 15

The beat ends with the party:

  1. Understanding the depth of Keeper history and sacrifice.
  2. Realizing that they are part of a much larger story—not just a quest, but a continuation of something sacred.
  3. Aware that Kaelen is carrying a terrible knowledge, though she is not sharing it.
  4. Rested and supplied for the final push.
  5. Pointing north toward Surt's Forge and the Emberwell, which is now visible in the distance.

Kaelen is no longer a reluctant guide. She is the inheritor of a tradition, the last of the Keepers, and she is walking toward her destiny.

The party may not fully understand this yet. But they understand that something has changed in her. She is more resolved, more focused, and more afraid.

GM Notes

📝 Kaelen's Arc: Layer 2

This is Layer 2—Kaelen now knows (or strongly suspects) that she is meant to become the Ever-Ember. This knowledge isolates her from the party. She carries it alone.

The discovery should be atmospheric. The archive is a place of learning and loss. Soren died here. The records are sacred. Treat this moment with weight.

Avoid being too explicit. The party should not fully understand Kaelen's revelation. She should drop hints, but not spell it out. This keeps the mystery alive until Beat 19.

📝 Atmosphere and Foreshadowing

The tower itself is a character. The Keeper wards, the warmth, the sense that knowledge and care still linger even in abandonment—these create a place that feels alive in its own way.

Mention Surt's Forge visibly. Make the distance feel manageable but urgent. The party should feel that they are close, but also that time is running out.

📝 Resources and Emotional Weight

Heat and resources: This is the last major resupply point before the final push. After this, Heat costs will increase significantly as the party moves into the mountains and the approaches to the Emberwell.

Word and Oaths: Remind the party of their oaths—to Rickety Frets, to the Jarl of Rindgate, possibly to others. These oaths are about to become very real. When the party succeeds (or fails), they will have to account for them.

The emotional toll: By this point in the campaign, the players should feel exhausted and emotionally heavy. They've lost people, seen horrors, and now they're carrying the weight of knowledge that Svellheim's fate rests on them. This is intentional. Let that weight sit.

Level: 5
Duration: 1 session
Location: Beinvaka (row 15, col 25); Pale Maw stronghold in the interior
Key NPCs: Pale Maw Inquisitor (fanatical leader), Pale Maw cultists, optional elder (less fanatical)
Type: Hard Montage Test (evasion/deception) OR Moderate-Hard Negotiation OR combat encounter, chase scene
Tone: Escalating tension; ideological enemies; the network is aware and coordinated; time pressure

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 45 | Level: 3 | VP into Level: 13

VP Status — Entering This Beat — Total VP (max): 45 | Level: 3 | VP into Level: 13

Overview

Beinvaka is no longer a neutral trading post. It has become a stronghold of the Pale Maw—the cult that believes the Fimbulwinter is correct, that the world should end, and that anyone trying to prevent it is committing heresy.

The party must pass through or around Beinvaka's territory. This is a challenging encounter because:

  1. The Pale Maw here are true believers, not mercenaries or network operatives. They cannot be bribed or reasoned with through appeals to survival or profit.
  2. They know the party is coming. Dreyfus's intelligence has propagated. Grafvitnir's network has had time to position defenders.
  3. They want Kaelen specifically. She is the Keeper, the heretic, the one trying to prevent the "correct" ending.

The beat presents the party with a choice: fight through, negotiate a passage, deceive the cultists, or go around (which costs time and Heat they can't afford).

By the end of this beat, the party is through Beinvaka's territory and heading north toward the Throat of World. They are exhausted, possibly injured, and keenly aware that Grafvitnir's pieces are in position.

Part 1: Approaching Beinvaka

The Settlement

Beinvaka (row 15, col 25) is described in the Atlas as "a nomad gathering-point on the western Bone-Fields, where Firekeepers light signal cairns during the new moon. It's not a town in any permanent sense, but there are always people here."

Before Fimbulwinter, it was neutral ground—a place where trade happened, where different groups could meet without hostility.

Now, it is a Pale Maw enclave. The signal cairns have been repurposed as watchtowers. The gathering-point has become a fortified camp.

As the party approaches, they see:

  • Defensive structures: Stone walls (partial), watchtowers, a central fire-pit that burns with an inverted flame (the Pale Maw believe in fire-worship, but they are heretical—they burn backwards, inverted, as if trying to negate warmth instead of create it).
  • Pale Maw cultists: Dozens of them, moving with purpose. They wear robes marked with symbols of entropy—spirals that collapse inward, stars with missing points, the signs of ending.
  • Refugees: A few people are camped outside the walls—locals who are trapped, waiting for shelter. They look terrified. Some are being "converted" to the Pale Maw ideology. Others are simply trying to survive.
  • Absence of normalcy: No markets, no friendly gathering. No music or laughter. This is a place of grim focus.

Detection and Approach

The party will be spotted. There is no way to approach Beinvaka without being seen.

The question is: What does the party do when they are spotted?

A Grafvitnir Pressure Check should have been made at the start of Beat 16. The roll determines the Pale Maw's readiness:

  • 1 = nothing: Pale Maw presence, but they're not specifically ready for the party. The party has a chance to bluff or negotiate without immediate pressure.
  • 2–4 = interference: Pale Maw are alert. A Pale Maw Inquisitor is here, with orders to intercept the party. But the response is not coordinated yet—the party has a window to act.
  • 5–6 = major intervention: Full mobilization. Pale Maw forces are positioned to intercept. The party's presence is expected. This becomes a hard fight or a very difficult evasion.

📝 Recommendation

Roll a 4–6 here. By Beat 16, Grafvitnir's network should be ahead of the party, not scrambling to catch up.

Part 2: The Confrontation

Initial Encounter with the Pale Maw

When the party is spotted, a delegation of Pale Maw come out to meet them. The delegation is led by Þráinn Ashward, Pale Maw Inquisitor.

Boss Encounter: Pale Maw Inquisitor

  • @UUID[Actor.OdxMlD7osKrRaOOG]{Pale Maw Inquisitor Thráinn} (Level 5 Boss)

Appearance: Tall, gaunt, with a visible scar across one eye (which they've made no effort to hide or heal). Robes of deep purple with silver thread depicting entropy symbols. Their manner is calm, certain, and completely uninterested in mercy.

Key Abilities:

  • Zealot's Judgment — Melee attack, marks target (next attack gains edge)
  • Voice of Entropy — Area 30ft, psychic damage + frightened (2 Malice)
  • Despair Aura — 20ft aura, allies +1 to Power Rolls, enemies test or −1 to next action
  • Absolute Conviction — Immune to fear and charm, edge on Presence tests vs mental effects
  • Heresy-Sense — Senses Kaelen within 60ft, edge on attacks targeting her
  • Final Requiem (3 Malice, below 15 Stamina) — 40ft, 2d6 psychic + stun

Personality: Clinical, analytical, certain in their purpose. Views the party as obstacles to be removed or converted. Kaelen they view as a heretic whose death would be a mercy.

When they meet the party, the Inquisitor speaks:

A figure steps forward from the fortified camp. Tall, scarred, draped in purple robes that seem to absorb light. Their eye—the one not covered by the scar—is fixed on the party with absolute certainty.

"I am Inquisitor Þráinn Ashward. You are the heretics sent to prevent the proper ending. You carry with you the last Keeper, the one who would kindle the false flame, who would deny the world its rest."

They pause, and their voice becomes almost gentle:

"I will make you an offer. Leave the Keeper with us. You may go. We will let you pass. You need not die for a losing cause."

Then, the smile fades:

"If you refuse, we will take her. And you will all die in the taking."

The Party's Options

Option 2: The Mad Dash (Hard Montage Test)

The party attempts to evade, deceive, or bluff their way through or around Beinvaka. This is a Hard Montage Test (2 rounds) representing the dangerous passage through the Pale Maw stronghold:

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 5 2

Round 1 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Deception or disguise Presence or Reason Convince the Pale Maw guards you are merchants, new converts, or travelers. The Inquisitor is watching—small mistakes reveal your true nature. Maintain the lie under pressure.
Navigate the settlement layout Intuition or Agility Scout the settlement mentally or physically. Find the northern exit. Identify where guards are posted. Plan a route that avoids the center fire-pit where the Inquisitor stands.
Keep Kaelen hidden or disguised Presence or Reason The Inquisitor has heresy-sense—they can feel Kaelen's presence. Mask her, hide her, make her seem unremarkable. The Inquisitor's attention is everything.

Round 1 Interlude: The Wrongness of Beinvaka

You move through the settlement, trying to seem unremarkable.

The Pale Maw cultists move with purpose, wearing robes marked with symbols of entropy—spirals that collapse inward, stars with missing points, the signs of ending. No music. No laughter. No market. This is a place of grim focus.

And the fire. The central fire-pit burns with an inverted flame—cold blues and sickly purples, as if the Pale Maw are trying to negate warmth instead of create it. The Inquisitor stands before it, their scar catching the unnatural light, their eye fixed on the party with absolute certainty.

You move quickly. Too quickly? No. No one stops you. Not yet.

But the Inquisitor's gaze follows. They sense something.

The Pale Maw are alert, but not yet mobilized. The party must move faster, push deeper into the settlement before alarms spread.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Misdirection and speed Agility or Intuition Move quickly through the settlement toward the northern exit. Avoid direct sight lines. Use structures for cover. Move like you belong here, even as you hurry.
Appeal to doubt or pragmatism Presence or Reason One of the Pale Maw younger recruits looks uncertain. A word in the right moment might slow pursuit. Plant doubt without revealing yourselves.
Keep the group moving together Presence or Might Cohesion is failing under pressure. Someone is afraid, slowing down. Push the group forward. Keep them moving. Keep them together.

Round 2 Interlude: Almost Caught

A guard looks directly at you.

Not a casual glance—a direct look, registering presence, beginning to question. Your heart stops.

Then, something miraculous happens.

The guard's gaze slides away. Their attention drifts. They turn and look in another direction—toward the southern approach, as if distracted by a sound that doesn't exist.

Did someone make a noise? Did Lew intentionally cause a distraction? Did luck simply favor you?

Behind you, the Inquisitor hasn't moved, but they're listening. Their scarred head turns slightly. They know something is happening. They just don't know what yet.

You have seconds. Maybe a minute.

The northern exit is visible now. 50 yards. Just 50 yards between you and the open road.

The party is almost clear. The Inquisitor is suspicious, but not yet certain. One more push could take them to safety.

Post-Montage Interlude: Escape and Pursuit

You run.

The Pale Maw don't chase immediately—they're shocked, uncertain. But the Inquisitor's voice rises, a psychic wail that echoes across the settlement:

"The heretic flees! The Keeper runs north! Mobilize! Mobilize!"

Cultists turn. Horns sound. But the party is already breaking through the northern gate, already at the edge of the settlement, already moving into the wilderness.

Behind you, figures move to pursue. A small team of riders begins to ride after you. But they won't chase far into open country—they're protecting the settlement, not the roads.

You're free. Bleeding. Exhausted. But free.

Ahead, the landscape opens into white and gray. The path north is clear. Surt's Forge is 2–3 days away.

And the Inquisitor's voice fades, but does not disappear. It echoes in your mind:

"We know where you're going. We will be waiting at the end."

The party has escaped, but the Pale Maw have sent word north. Grafvitnir knows they're coming. The net is closing.

Result Outcome

Option 3: Negotiation with the Pale Maw Elder (Moderate-Hard)

Only if the party is very creative or if they roleplay creatively from the start, or if they fail the Montage Test and seek an alternative.

There may be a Pale Maw elder—someone less fanatical than the Inquisitor, someone who joined the movement for protection rather than ideology. If the party can reach this person (away from the Inquisitor's direct sight) and make the right appeals, they might negotiate passage or create a distraction.

This is a Moderate-Hard Negotiation Encounter.

Negotiation Setup

Interest: 1 (Exhausted) — The elder has joined the Pale Maw for survival and protection, not ideology. They are weary, conflicted, and beginning to doubt whether the end of the world is truly desirable. They see the party as an unexpected complication.

Patience: 2 — The elder is frightened of the Inquisitor and cannot afford to be seen speaking at length with outsiders. Their time is limited. If the party cannot make a compelling case quickly, the elder will withdraw.

📝 Draw Steel Negotiation Mechanics

Under Draw Steel rules, each argument is a power roll using Reason, Intuition, or Presence (with any applicable interpersonal skill). Motivations require a medium difficulty roll; arguments with no motivation or pitfall require a difficult roll.

Motivation appeal (medium difficulty):

  • Tier 1 (11 or lower): Patience −1. No Interest change.
  • Tier 2 (12–16): Interest +1, Patience −1.
  • Tier 3 (17+): Interest +1, Patience unchanged.

No motivation or pitfall (difficult):

  • Tier 1: Patience −1, Interest −1.
  • Tier 2: Patience −1 only.
  • Tier 3: Interest +1, Patience −1. (Natural 19–20: Patience unchanged.)

Pitfall invoked: Automatic failure. Interest −1, Patience −1.

With Patience 2 and Interest 1, the party is negotiating from a very weak position. They need to reach Interest 3 to get meaningful help (passage or a distraction). One Tier 3 appeal and one Tier 2 appeal can do it, or two consecutive Tier 3s. One pitfall severely damages chances of success. This is a narrow window, and the elder is terrified—speed and precision matter.

Each motivation can only be successfully invoked once.

Motivations (medium difficulty)

Motivation Appeal
Survival Appeal to the elder's desire to live. Frame it not as ideology, but pragmatism: "The world continues if we succeed. Your world continues. You can have a life after this, a real life, not one lived in fear of the Inquisitor's judgment." The elder joined the Pale Maw because the Fimbulwinter seemed inevitable, a protection against despair. If the party can offer a concrete alternative—a future worth living in—it speaks to something the elder thought was dead.
Doubt About the Movement The elder is already conflicted. They can sense that true belief, real conviction, eluded them when they joined. Appeal to this doubt directly: "The Fimbulwinter cannot be stopped by faith alone. If renewal is possible, don't you want to see it? Don't you want to know if there's a chance?" The elder is not a zealot. They are a frightened person who made a survival choice. Validation of that doubt is powerful.
Pragmatism Appeal to cold reason and self-interest. "The Inquisitor serves a god that may not exist. We serve a world that does. If the world continues, your life continues. Isn't that worth something?" The elder respects logic over emotion. A practical argument—that their own life is better if the party succeeds—can move them.

Pitfalls (automatic failure — Interest −1, Patience −1)

⚠ Negotiation Pitfalls

  • Defending the Ever-Ember or attacking the Pale Maw faith directly: "Your god is a lie" or "The Fimbulwinter is wrong." The elder is not ready for this. They are frightened and half-committed. A direct assault on their beliefs makes them defensive and likely to call for the Inquisitor.
  • Claiming the Fimbulwinter should be stopped: "We're going to stop the Fimbulwinter." The elder hears this as naive heresy. They see you as exactly the kind of dangerous idealist the Inquisitor warned about.
  • Mentioning saving the world: "We're going to save the world." The elder dismisses this as fantasy. "That's not possible. You don't understand what you're facing." The elder withdraws, confidence shaken.
  • Revealing Kaelen's identity: If the party reveals Kaelen is the Keeper, or the Inquisitor learns of the conversation, the elder must inform the Inquisitor or face punishment. Negotiation fails immediately. The Inquisitor will move to intercept the party.

Negotiation Flow

The party must reach the elder discreetly, away from the Inquisitor's sight. This might involve finding them in the settlement, perhaps during a moment when they are isolated. The elder is nervous and will speak quickly, aware of the danger.

  • Presence: Emotional appeals and empathy work if they avoid preaching. Appeal to the elder's humanity, their desire to live, their fatigue with ideology.
  • Reason: Logical arguments about self-interest and pragmatism. The elder respects someone who cuts through moral posturing.
  • Intuition: Reading the elder's state of mind. They are terrified and conflicted. Intuition can help identify which motivations will resonate most strongly.

Reading the Elder's Responses

Use these to guide roleplay between arguments:

  • Interest goes up: The elder's shoulders relax slightly. They look around nervously, then back at the party. "You're... you're right about that." Their voice becomes quieter, more earnest. Hope flickers—dangerous, but real.
  • Interest stays flat (no-motivation argument): The elder shakes their head. "That doesn't help me. That's just words." They look over their shoulder. The fear returns. "I don't have time for this."
  • Interest drops (pitfall): The elder's face hardens. They step back. "No. I won't listen to heresy. I can't." They look around for the Inquisitor, panicked. The negotiation is collapsing.
  • Patience at 1: The elder stands abruptly. "I have to go. The Inquisitor will notice. I'm sorry." They are about to leave. If the party has made any progress (Interest 2 or higher), the elder will return after dark or agree to a quick distraction. If Interest is still 1, they flee and will not engage again.

Negotiation Outcome: Success

If the party succeeds in raising the elder's Interest to 3 or higher, the elder offers help:

"I will... I will create a distraction when you try to leave. Not much. A fire in the eastern stables, perhaps, or a false alarm. It won't hold for long, but it will give you time to reach the northern road. That's all I can do. After that, you're on your own."

This gives the party a concrete advantage—a brief window to break through or slip away. It's not a guarantee of success, but it significantly improves their odds.

Option 4: Going Around (Costs Heat and Time)

The party can abandon the direct northern route and go around Beinvaka—east through the foothills, then north. This adds 40+ miles and 2-3 days to their journey.

The Heat cost is severe (extreme cold, difficult terrain, no shelter).

The party might choose this if they are exhausted and want to avoid combat entirely. It's an option, but it costs them time they don't have.

Part 3: Grafvitnir Pressure Resolution

Regardless of how the party gets through Beinvaka, the outcome is the same:

Grafvitnir's network is now fully coordinated.

The Pale Maw here will send a message north (via courier or Wyrd-channel) that the party is coming, that they did not stop them, and that the party is heading for Surt's Forge and the Emberwell.

Grafvitnir now knows, with certainty, that the party is 2-3 days from their goal.

The defenses at the Emberwell will be ready.

Kaelen's Hardening

After the confrontation at Beinvaka, Kaelen becomes very quiet. She has faced the ideology that wants her dead—or worse, wants her to accept that her life's work is wrong, that the Keepers are heretics, that the Fimbulwinter is correct.

As the party moves north, away from Beinvaka, Kaelen speaks softly:

"Whatever I become at the Emberwell, it will be the opposite of what they want. They want the world to end. I will make sure it continues."

She pauses, her hand touching the ember-mark on her forehead.

"That is what the Keeper-oath is—a defiance of the Fimbulwinter. A commitment to warmth and life, no matter the cost."

Her voice becomes steady, almost grim:

"I do not know what waits for me at the Emberwell. But I know that I am not afraid anymore. Not of the Pale Maw. Not of the Fimbulwinter. Not even of what I might become."

She looks at the party:

"Thank you for believing in me. When we reach the Emberwell, you will understand why that matters."

She is more resolved now. More hardened. More willing to accept what comes next.

Part 4: The Path North

Exiting Beinvaka Territory

Once the party gets through or around Beinvaka, they are on the northern road. The terrain begins to change:

  • The interior plateau gives way to lower hills.
  • The landscape becomes more barren.
  • The air is colder, sharper.
  • In the distance, mountains become visible—the northern reaches, the high plateaus that lead to the White Waste.

The party is now 2-3 days from Surt's Forge.

Heat is extremely low. They cannot afford to rest long or to take detours.

Optional Encounter: Pale Maw Pursuit (Brief)

If the party fought or evaded dramatically, a small Pale Maw pursuit team (4-6 riders/walkers) might follow them for a few hours. But they will not chase too far into the wilderness. They retreat to Beinvaka after a few miles.

This can be a brief combat encounter (easy to moderate) or simply a scene where the party sees them falling behind and feels a moment of triumph that they survived.

GM Notes

📝 Pacing and Tone

This beat can be quick (30 minutes) if the party bluffs through, or a full session if combat erupts or negotiation is attempted. The Pale Maw are not mercenaries—they cannot be bought or reasoned with. They are true believers. Treat them with the gravitas of an ideological enemy.

Make it clear that Grafvitnir's network is working as a unified force now. The Pale Maw, the undead, the scouts—they are all moving together.

📝 Ideal Outcome

The party should get through with costs (exhaustion, injuries, or resources spent) but without total victory. They should feel that they escaped, but barely. Kaelen's hardening is crucial: she's moving from reluctant questioner to determined leader.

Time pressure is critical. This is the last major obstacle before the final two beats. The party should feel that time is running out.

⚠ Oaths Come Due

The party's oaths are accumulating weight. Remind the players of their growing obligations:

  • Gragnir's Root-Oath (Beat 8): Serve Yggdrasil's renewal above all other obligations — above kings, councils, and politics. This conflicts with their Konungs-ferð commitment to Harald.
  • Rickety Frets's Three Demands (Beat 12): Remember him. Send word back. Protect or destroy the Bundingsteinn if they find it.
  • Vigmund's Advocacy Oath (Beat 13): Send word back. Advocate for Rindgate's refugees at Hrafnborg — fight for their shelter at the council table.
  • The Archive Oath (Beat 15): Keeper knowledge used only to restore the Ever-Embers. Not as a weapon against the living. Not shared with those who serve the Grave-Wolf.

These oaths are not decorative. The Word economy enforces each one, and several pull in different directions. When the party reaches the Emberwell, they will be tested.

Overview

The Steam-Oasis is the last place of real warmth before the Throat of World and the approach to Emberwell. It is a geothermal vent complex in the northern interior (row 12, col 27), where the ground itself is warm enough to melt snow and grow twisted, hardy flora.

This beat serves several purposes:

  1. Provides the party a final rest and resupply opportunity before the climactic final beats.
  2. Offers a moment of strange beauty and temptation in a world of cold and ice.
  3. Allows for a Hard Montage Test representing the dangerous blizzard crossing of the Throat of World.
  4. Builds atmosphere and anticipation for the final approach to the Emberwell.

By the end of this beat, the party has rested, resupplied, and committed to the final push. They have faced the Throat of World and are standing at the entrance to Surt's Forge, visible in the distance.

Part 1: The Approach to the Steam-Oasis

The Transition Zone

The party is traveling north from Beinvaka. The landscape is becoming increasingly barren and cold:

  • Snow is deeper here—2-3 feet in places.
  • Vegetation has disappeared almost entirely.
  • The sun, even at midday, hangs low and provides little heat.
  • The wind is constant and brutal.

This is the transition into the true north.

Heat is critical now. The party should feel they are approaching the limit of what their supplies can sustain.

Then, after a day of hard travel, they begin to notice something:

  • The air is warmer, very slightly.
  • The snow is melting in patches.
  • There are green things—actual living plants, twisted and small, but alive.
  • Steam rises from the ground in places, carrying a mineral smell.

The Oasis Itself

The Steam-Oasis is a circular area about 200 yards across, centered on a geothermal vent complex. The geological features include:

  • Central vent: A large opening in the ground where steam rises constantly. The ground around it is too hot to touch.
  • Mineral pools: Circles of water warmed by the vent, colored strange shades of blue, yellow, and rust by dissolved minerals. The water is drinkable but has a strong mineral taste.
  • Flora: Bizarre, alien plants have adapted to this place. Stunted trees with bark the color of rust. Shrubs with leaves of deep blue and silver. Grasses that grow in twisted spirals. A few flowers, impossibly delicate.
  • Shelter: There may or may not be structures here (GM choice). Either the party finds a natural cave or lean-to, or they find abandoned structures (old stone buildings or shelter-huts) that previous inhabitants left behind.

The warmth hits you like a physical thing.

For the first time in weeks, the cold is not the dominant sensation. Your breath doesn't immediately freeze. Your fingers don't burn with frostbite pain.

The landscape is alien. Living green grows here—real, living plants—in a landscape where everything else has been brown and white and gray for hundreds of miles. It's beautiful and deeply wrong at the same time.

The ground steams. Water bubbles up from cracks in the earth, carrying minerals and heat. The smell is sharp, iron-rich, almost unbearable at first. But after a moment, it smells like something alive. Like the earth has a heartbeat.

And the light here is strange. The steam catches the pale sun and refracts it, creating halos and prisms. It's dreamlike. It's easy to imagine that you've left the real world and entered something from myth.

Kaelen stands at the edge of the warm zone, feeling the warmth on her face. She closes her eyes.

"This is what the north felt like, before the Fimbulwinter. My grandmother told me stories. This is what she described."

Then her eyes open again, and the realization returns:

"This can't last. It's geothermal. The heat will fail within a few years, maybe less. And when it does... this place will be gone. Everything that lives here will die."

Inhabitants (Optional)

The Steam-Oasis is small. It cannot support a large population. But a small community may have gathered here—people who chose to stay in the warmth rather than flee south.

The Oasis Community

A small community of 20-50 people, mostly refugees from the interior. They have made a deliberate choice to stay here, knowing the resource is finite. They are not hostile, but they are deeply suspicious of outsiders.

Social interaction:

  • They welcome respectful visitors cautiously and offer shelter, warm food, and information about Surt's Forge.
  • If Kaelen is identified as a Keeper, they recognize her and react with fear, hope, or desperation.

Their dilemma: They might ask: "What are you doing going north? Why would anyone go north?" Or, if they sense what Kaelen is, they understand: "You're going to try to end the Fimbulwinter. We hope you succeed. But we know you might fail."

Tone: This should be a scene (30-45 minutes of table time) that adds emotional weight and creates a genuine moral dilemma.

The Oath of Silence

Once the party has been welcomed and fed, the community's leader — a grey-haired woman called @UUID[Actor.PjIcqf3pfBREHqV4]{Elder Thyra} — draws them aside. She speaks plainly.

Thyra's hands are callused from work, not from weapons. She is not a warrior. She is a farmer who learned to lead because someone had to.

"You are welcome here. You may rest, eat, resupply. We will tell you what we know about the forge to the north and the dead that walk the passes. This costs you nothing."

She pauses. Her eyes are steady.

"But I need something from you in return. Not payment. An oath."

"You will not tell anyone — not your King, not your allies, not the refugees on the road — where this place is. You will not describe the route. You will not draw maps. You will not mention the steam, the warmth, or the water. If someone asks where you rested, you say 'a cave.' If they press, you say nothing."

She leans forward.

"I have forty-three people here. Twenty of them are children. The geothermal vents will keep us alive for three years, maybe four, if we are careful. If word spreads — if refugees come in numbers — we will be overrun in months. Not by enemies. By desperate, starving, freezing people who need exactly what we have. And when they come, the food runs out in weeks. The water becomes fouled. The children die first, because they always do."

"So I am asking you to let us survive. Swear silence, and everything we have is open to you. Refuse, and you may still rest here tonight — I am not cruel — but you leave at dawn with nothing but what you carried in."

If the party accepts the Oath of Silence: They gain full hospitality — rest, resupply (5 days' rations, warm gear repairs, medicine), and detailed intelligence about the route to Surt's Forge. @UUID[Actor.PjIcqf3pfBREHqV4]{Elder Thyra} also shares a critical piece of information: there is a second geothermal source near the forge itself, which may be relevant to the Emberwell.

If the party refuses: They rest one night (recovering Stamina), but gain no supplies, no intelligence, and no special information. @UUID[Actor.PjIcqf3pfBREHqV4]{Elder Thyra} is not angry — she is resigned. "I understand. You have other promises to keep. I hope they are worth more than ours."

⚠ Director Warning: The Oath Conflict with Vigmund

This is the campaign's first genuinely painful oath collision. Read carefully.

If the party swore Vigmund's refugee advocacy oath in Beat 13, they are now trapped:

  • Vigmund's oath: The party swore to advocate for Rindgate's refugees — to argue for their shelter and survival at Hrafnborg. Knowledge of the Steam-Oasis (a warm, habitable, self-sustaining refuge) would be the single most valuable thing they could offer Vigmund's people. The Oasis could save hundreds of lives.
  • Thyra's oath: The party swears never to reveal the Oasis's location. Keeping this oath means deliberately withholding survival information from the refugees they promised to help.

There is no clean resolution:

  • Keep the silence oath, break the advocacy oath: Vigmund's refugees may die or suffer unnecessarily because the party knows about a refuge and said nothing. The party loses 1 Word permanently for failing Vigmund.
  • Break the silence oath, keep the advocacy oath: Thyra's community is overrun. Forty-three people — twenty of them children — lose their only chance at survival. The party loses 1 Word permanently for betraying Thyra's trust.
  • Try to find a middle path: The party might argue for relocating Vigmund's refugees to Hrafnborg (as originally promised) while keeping the Oasis secret. This is the most ethical path, but it is also the hardest — Hrafnborg is under siege, resources are scarce, and the party will be arguing for admission to a city that may not want more mouths to feed.

Do not resolve this for the players. Let them sit with it. The Word economy enforces the cost either way. This is what oaths mean in Svellheim.

Alternatively: The Oasis is empty. No one has come here in months. The shelter is abandoned, but useful. The party rests in silence, alone in a place of warmth and wonder.

Either way, the tone is bittersweet: warmth and beauty in a dying world.

Part 2: Rest and Resupply

Heat and Healing

The Oasis is warm. Resting here reduces Heat cost to nearly zero—the geothermal warmth does most of the work.

The party can recover Stamina fully with a night's rest. This is their last opportunity to do so before the final beats.

Supplies

The Oasis has:

  • Fresh water: In abundance, warm and mineral-rich.
  • Flora: The local plants are edible (bitter, mineral-tasting, but nutritious). The party can harvest enough for several days of rations.
  • Fuel: Dead wood from fallen trees. Easy to gather.

The party can resupply and prepare adequately for the final push.

A Quiet Moment

The party has settled in to rest. The warmth of the geothermal vents surrounds them for the first time in weeks. As evening settles, one of the party members might notice something quiet happening near one of the larger thermal vents.

Kaelen is sitting near the warm stone, her hands tracing something into the mineral dust on the ground. Root-runes, maybe. An old habit from her training. Her movements are careful, meditative—not prayer, exactly, but something like remembrance.

Lew is nearby. Not sitting with her, just… nearby. Close enough to see clearly, close enough to respond if something goes wrong, far enough to give her space. His attention, when it settles on her, is steady and complete.

If anyone notices, it is because they are paying very close attention. Lew is not watching her in the way you might expect a companion to watch a companion. He is watching her the way you might watch something precious and fragile that you know you are going to lose.

When he sees that he has been noticed, he does not deflect. He simply says, "Making sure she doesn't wander. She does that." It is not a lie, exactly. But it is not the whole truth either.

If Kaelen is asked directly, she will glance at Lew with complete affection and say: "Lew worries about everyone. It's one of his more exhausting qualities." She says it with no self-consciousness whatsoever.

⚠ GM Secret: Watching What He Cannot Keep

This is a small moment. A man noticing that someone matters to him, and allowing his notice to be visible to a stranger—because he does not know when he will have another chance to simply watch her, alive and warm and present. He will not speak of this to Kaelen. He will not cross any lines. But the watching is its own kind of goodbye, even though goodbye is not yet.

Temptation (Optional GM Move)

The Oasis is warm. Safe. Stable. A character might suggest: "We could stay here. Hole up. Wait for spring. It's coming, eventually."

This is temptation. Kaelen, if asked, will say firmly:

"No. The Oasis is warming because of geothermal activity, not because the Fimbulwinter is ending. In a year, maybe less, this place will start to fail. And Svellheim can't wait. The Keepers are dead. Hrafnborg is starving. The Emberwell has to be renewed now, or the world ends in a few years."

She pauses, then adds:

"Besides, if we stop here, we've come all this way for nothing. We've already paid the price in lives, in sacrifice, in oaths sworn. We can't stop now."

The party will choose to continue (because the narrative momentum demands it, and because they know the cost of stopping).

Part 2.5: The Heitfolk (Optional Side Quest)

The Scout's Approach

As the party travels north from the Steam-Oasis, moving through the mountain foothills toward the Throat of World, they notice movement on a high ledge—a small, compact figure watching them from perhaps 40 feet above. The figure does not flee, but neither does it approach. It is clearly observing the party, deciding whether they are a threat or potential allies.

If the party acts non-threateningly (sheathing weapons, speaking calmly, showing no aggression), the figure will eventually descend. This is @UUID[Actor.EqbUegeprUzE1G7R]{Bolverkt} (literally "trouble-work" in the old tongue—she was named this because she was expected to cause problems and has exceeded all expectations).

Bolverkt is a Djúpdvergr (Deep-Dwarf) of perhaps 45 years old—young to middle-aged for her people. She is compact and dark-featured, with skin the color of deep umber, her features weathered by decades in firelight. She carries a forged axe of obvious quality and wears armor that, while ancient, is meticulously maintained.

Her accent is thick, her manner blunt to the point of rudeness, but there is no malice in her—only impatience.

"You're headed north. That's stupid. But you're moving with purpose and you've got an ember-mark with you, and that's not stupid, so maybe you're just locally stupid rather than cosmically stupid."

She studies Kaelen with frank intensity.

"You're a Keeper, aren't you? Or what's left of them. We thought your kind was all dead in the Long Night."

She doesn't wait for an answer before continuing.

"I'm Bolverkt. I scout for Djúphol—that's the Deep-Hall, under the northern mountains, and the only place north of here that isn't trying actively to kill you. The Heitfolk live there. We've lived there for centuries while the surface world forgot we existed."

She pauses, and something that might almost be a smile crosses her face.

"The problem is, we're dying. Slowly. And if you're going north to do what I think you're going to do, you might be the solution. Or the last witnesses to our ending. Either way, you should come."

The Offer and the Conditions

@UUID[Actor.EqbUegeprUzE1G7R]{Bolverkt} will guide the party to Djúphol (the Deep-Hall) if they agree to two conditions:

  1. No weapons drawn inside the settlement. The Heitfolk are not used to outsiders. A drawn sword will trigger panic and possibly violence. The party must trust that they are safe.
  2. They speak only to Hildvarð Bruneson, the Forgemaster. The settlement is not prepared for open contact. Bolverkt doesn't want rumors spreading or chaos. The Forgemaster will decide what information is shared.

If the party agrees, Bolverkt leads them via hidden mountain passages (bypassing both the Throat of World and the worst of the surface crossing). The journey takes approximately 4-5 hours of steady travel through carved tunnels that are clearly ancient and carefully maintained.

Djúphol: The Deep-Hall

The tunnel opens into warmth.

Not gradually—suddenly. You step from cold stone passage into air that is warm, thick with moisture, heavy with the smell of hot metal and deep stone. The light changes too. Forge-light—orange and flickering, casting dancing shadows on carved stone walls. It smells alive.

The tunnel opens into a vast chamber, perhaps 200 feet across and 60 feet high, its ceiling lost in darkness above the reach of the firelight. The floor is carved smooth by countless feet, and structures line the walls: workshops, homes carved into the stone itself, all arranged in careful, precise order.

In the center of the chamber: three massive geothermal vents, heat rising in visible waves. The floor around them is scorched black. And in the center of the chamber: the Forge-Hall—a vast forge-complex, equipped with ancient tools and equipment of obvious mastercraft. Anvils. Bellows. Equipment for work that will never be completed.

People emerge to watch you pass. They are compact, dark-featured, their skin ranging from deep umber to grey-blue. Children cling to adults. The adults' expressions range from curiosity to fear. They recognize the ember-mark. They know what it means.

The warmth is incredible. For the first time since leaving Hrafnborg, the party is genuinely warm. The cold, which has been a constant companion, is absent. The comfort of it is almost painful—a reminder of what the world was like before the Fimbulwinter.

At the far end of the chamber, before the largest forge, stands a single figure. Massive by dwarf standards, with a face lined by age and grief. His eyes are focused and certain. This is @UUID[Actor.VDWwMniqZfw6wdMm]{Hildvarð} Bruneson, Forgemaster of Djúphol.

Hildvarð Bruneson

Age: 140 years old (middle-aged for a dwarf, but carrying the weight of decades of loss)

Appearance: Massive and quiet. Wears the practical clothes of a smith—apron stained with decades of work, hands calloused and scarred by fire. His face is lined but not weak. His eyes are the most alive thing about him—observant, clear, forming conclusions that he acts on without explanation.

Manner: Speaks rarely. When he does, it is with precision and authority. He does not waste words. He treats the party with respect but no warmth. This is not coldness, but practicality.

Secret Grief: His son Bruni died three years ago when a lower vent collapsed—a cave-in he is directly responsible for failing to prevent. No one speaks of this, but it explains the bleakness that lives in his eyes. It explains why he will help the party—because he has already lost everything that mattered, and helping might give that loss meaning.

The Greeting

@UUID[Actor.VDWwMniqZfw6wdMm]{Hildvarð} approaches the party without ceremony. He does not bow or offer greeting gestures. He simply stops before them, regarding Kaelen directly.

"You are the ones heading north. The ember-mark told us you were coming. We have felt you moving toward us through the deep places. The roots carry your warmth like a signal."

He pauses, his eyes never leaving Kaelen's face.

"We have a problem. Your presence here suggests you have a solution. This is an exchange, not charity. I prefer exchanges. Charity implies pity, and I have no use for pity."

He gestures to the Forge-Hall, to the settlement beyond.

"The Heitfolk have survived by staying invisible. We have survived by maintaining what the First Keepers built—the vent-network, the warmth-ways that thread through the deep mountain. But the warmth is failing. The roots are failing. Something is poisoning them from deeper down. We call it the Rot. We have no idea what it truly is, only that it is consuming the deep places, and if it reaches the primary vents, Djúphol will become a tomb."

He continues, his voice never wavering.

"I believe you know what the Rot is. I believe you are going north to confront it. I have records—iron-plate records, carved by the First Keepers themselves—that describe how the old Keepers could redirect the root-warmth, could guide the deep fires away from corruption. You have the mark. You have the ability. That is what I see."

He steps closer, and when he speaks, his voice is very quiet.

"If you use that ability to help us, I will give you safe passage through the Heitfolk tunnel network. The tunnels emerge 12 miles north of where the Throat of World exits. On the sheltered side. This reduces your crossing time and spares you the worst of the blizzard. You will arrive at the threshold of Surt's Forge rested instead of broken."

He waits, then adds: "But the work will exhaust you. The Keeper who walks the lower passages will need to spend Recoveries to complete the task. I will not lie about the cost. If you agree, understand what you are agreeing to."

The Lower Passages: A Montage Test (Moderate Difficulty)

Victory Points — Type: Montage Test (Optional - Heitfolk side quest) | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on success / 0 VP on failure (2 rounds)

Victory Points — Type: Montage Test (Optional - Heitfolk side quest) | Difficulty: Moderate | VP Award: 1 VP on success / 0 VP on failure (2 rounds)

If the party agrees, Kaelen will lead them into the lower passages of Djúphol, into the infected sections where the Rot is thick and active. This is presented as a Montage Test (Moderate, 2 rounds) with different character mechanics than typical survival montages—this is repair and restoration under pressure.

The Heitfolk provide maps carved into iron plates. The party must follow these maps, help Kaelen navigate the corrupted sections, protect her while she does dangerous work, and help her redirect the root-warmth back into healthy vent channels.

Challenge Characteristic Description
Clearing blockages Might or Reason The Rot has created physical blockages in the vent passages—tangled, blackened root-matter that is actively resisting. Physically clear the blockage or reason out a way to redirect flow around it. The work is heavy and repulsive.
Reading the vent network Reason or Intuition The iron-plate maps show the vent system, but the Rot has warped things. Understand the network's geometry, predict flow, anticipate where the blockages will create dangerous pressure buildups. One mistake could collapse a passage with everyone inside it.
Guiding Kaelen around the worst corruption Intuition or Agility The Rot is thick in some places, thin in others. Sense where it is thickest and guide Kaelen around it. The ember-mark hurts when exposed to concentrated corruption. Keep her path as clear as possible.
Keeping Kaelen grounded and focused Presence or Intuition The work is painful. The corruption tries to pull her attention, tries to make her falter. Keep her steady. Keep her focused. Keep her believing that the work will succeed. Speak to her strength, not her fear.

Round 1: Descending Into Corruption

The lower passages are nothing like the Forge-Hall. The air here is thick, almost liquid, and it tastes wrong—sulfurous and sweet and rotten all at once. The light from your torches seems to be absorbed here, creating shadows that seem deeper than they should be.

The walls are scarred with black. The root-matter that lines the passages has turned black, and it writhes slightly—not with life, but with something that mimics life. It's obscene to look at directly.

Kaelen steps forward, and her ember-mark flares. It's a reaction of instinct, a rejection of what surrounds her. She closes her eyes for a moment, gathering herself, then continues.

"This is what I can feel. This is what the world tastes like from inside the roots. This is the Rot working, consuming, replacing living wood with this... this mockery."

She opens her eyes, and they burn with amber light.

"Let's get to work."

Round 1 successes mean the party clears the first major blockage and establishes a safe working area. Round 1 failures mean the passage becomes unstable, forcing a careful retreat and choosing an alternate route (losing time and resources).

Interlude: The Forge-Shrine

Deep in the second passage, far from the Forge-Hall, the party finds a small shrine. The Heitfolk place these throughout their tunnels—offerings to the heat, to the deep powers, to the stone itself.

This one is still warm. An offering rests at its center: a small iron figure, crudely made, clearly the work of a child. It is still here. The Rot has not reached this place. Not yet.

Someone made this. A child, by the look of it. Someone in this mountain took time, in a place where time might be running out, to make something beautiful. Something small and imperfect and full of hope.

And it's still here. Still warm. Still offered.

It breaks something in the silence of the moment.

Round 2: Rerouting the Flow

The final stage of the work: Kaelen must place her hands on the corrupted root-channels and pull the warmth away from the Rot-infected sections, guiding it into clean channels, back toward the healthy vents.

This is painful. Her ember-mark burns white-hot. The party witnesses her gasping, her hands trembling, but her will holding firm.

On success (more successes than failures), she reroutes 2 of the 3 critical warmth-channels. The Heitfolk's vents will run warm for approximately another year—buying them time to either find a permanent solution or prepare to abandon the settlement.

On failure (more failures than successes), she manages only 1 channel. The Heitfolk will have months, not years. @UUID[Actor.VDWwMniqZfw6wdMm]{Hildvarð}'s expression hardens further, but he honors the agreement nonetheless. He is, above all, practical.

The Cost

Kaelen spends 2 Recoveries to complete this work. When the montage ends, she is exhausted—drained in a way that normal rest will not immediately repair. She will recover, but not until after a full night's sleep in a place of safety.

⚠ GM Secret: Kaelen's Growing Understanding

Each time Kaelen uses her ember-mark to do difficult work, she understands more clearly what the Binding means. She has felt the world through the roots. She has felt the corruption and resisted it. She understands, more than ever, that the Keeper's role is not to manage flame, but to become the boundary between corruption and continuation.

She will not speak of this understanding to the party until Beat 19.

Hildvarð's Farewell

When the party emerges from the lower passages, exhausted and shaken, @UUID[Actor.VDWwMniqZfw6wdMm]{Hildvarð} greets them at the threshold. He looks at Kaelen with something that is almost—not quite—emotion.

He grips her arm. This is the closest the Heitfolk come to showing deep gratitude or respect. No words. Just the grip, firm and certain, then released.

He turns to the rest of the party and says simply: "You carried her down. You brought her back. That is all that can be asked. The tunnels ahead are safe. They emerge on the northern approach. Bolverkt will guide you to the exit. Beyond that is Surt's Forge. Beyond that is whatever you are going to do."

Then he adds, as an afterthought: "When you reach the Forge, know that the Heitfolk are watching. Not with eyes. But through the deep places. We will know if you succeed. We will know if you fail."

The Tunnel Passage and the Gift

@UUID[Actor.EqbUegeprUzE1G7R]{Bolverkt} guides the party through the Heitfolk tunnels toward the northern exit. The journey is warm, safe, and fast—a stark contrast to the surface crossing they will soon undertake.

The tunnels are ancient and maintained with meticulous care. The walls are carved with precision, the floor smooth and level. Torches burn in sconces, fed by oil that Bolverkt explains is rendered from deep-dwelling fungi that the Heitfolk cultivate.

You pass through chambers where families work—smithing, weaving, preserving food. Children stop and stare at you. Adults nod respectfully. There is no fear here, only quiet observation.

The air is warm throughout. The stone thrums with a deep, distant vibration—the heartbeat of the mountain, the pulse of the vents that keep this place alive.

As you walk, Bolverkt speaks more freely than she did before.

"Does the sun really shine like the old stories say? We have records, carved on plates, but no one alive has seen it. My grandmother's grandmother saw it once, before the Fimbulwinter. She couldn't describe it well enough for it to mean anything to us. How do you describe light without shadow to people who have lived their whole lives in firelight?"

She glances back at the party, and there is something almost vulnerable in her expression.

"If you succeed, if the Fimbulwinter truly ends, will you come back and tell us about the sun? So we can tell our children?"

The party exits the tunnels approximately 12 miles north of the Throat of World's exit point, and on the sheltered side of the mountain ridge. The weather is harsh, but not as brutal as it would have been on the exposed pass.

The Gift From Hildvarð

Before the party departs the Deep-Hall, @UUID[Actor.VDWwMniqZfw6wdMm]{Hildvarð} presents them with a final gift: @UUID[Item.2d3nqTnvxREshnZ2]{Hildvarð's Sealed Iron Canteen}, forged-welded shut, containing concentrated geothermal heat.

Hildvarð's Sealed Iron Canteen

Properties:

  • Contains enough warmth to keep one person comfortable through a full night in the open without consuming any Heat resource.
  • When opened (only at a moment of dire need), releases enough heat to warm an entire small space (a shelter, a cave, a tent) for several hours.
  • Once opened, the seal cannot be reforged. It is a one-use resource.
  • Forged with Heitfolk craftsmanship. It is beautiful, even sealed. Carved with symbols of hearth-fires and deep places.

The Gift: @UUID[Actor.VDWwMniqZfw6wdMm]{Hildvarð} places it directly into Kaelen's hands. He says nothing except: "For the keeper. When you need to remember what warmth is, when you need to remember what you're fighting for."

Part 3: The Throat of World Passage

The Geography

The Throat of World (row 9, col 23) is a mountain pass that connects the interior plateau to the far northern reaches where the White Waste begins. It is:

  • Narrow: Perhaps 200 yards across at its widest point, between two ridges that rise to 40+ feet high.
  • Exposed: No shelter, no protection from wind.
  • Notorious for blizzards: The pass channels wind and creates localized whiteout conditions.
  • The last barrier: Beyond the Throat lies the true north—Surt's Forge, the Emberwell, and the highest reaches of Svellheim.

The Blizzard Montage (Hard Montage Test)

Round 1 Sample Tests

Round 1 Interlude: The Blizzard Closes In

The blizzard hits like a hand closing.

One moment, you can see the path ahead, the dark rocks of the Throat of World visible against the white sky. The next moment, snow is everywhere, moving horizontally, driven by wind that tries to tear you backward.

Visibility drops to arm's length. Then to chest-length. Then you're walking blind, feeling your way forward by rope and faith.

The wind screams. It's not wind. It sounds like something vast and hungry, trying to swallow you whole.

Your breath freezes in your chest. Your eyes water—ice crystals form on your eyelashes. Your fingers, even in gloves, burn with cold that feels like fire.

And all around you: white. Nothing but white. The world has ended and been replaced with this—this killing cold, this endless storm, this place where no living thing is meant to go.

The blizzard is not a simple difficulty. It is a malevolent force, trying to separate, freeze, and kill the party. Minute by minute, the cold is draining warmth, draining strength, draining hope.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
